engineGuide.h2: Mercedes-Benz A Class Engine Codes - Full Technical Specifications & Replacement Guide
engineGuide.h3: Every engine code fitted to the Mercedes-Benz A Class across all generations, with technical specs, compatible variants and trims, known failure points, and average rebuilt prices from UK specialists. Select any engine code to compare quotes.

entries.title: 1.7L Diesel
entries.history: The OM668 was Mercedes' first common-rail diesel for the A-Class, introduced with the W168. It offered CDI efficiency in a compact transverse package .
commonFailures: Injector seal leaks leading to combustion gas blow-by
Glow plug corrosion seizing in cylinder head
entries.cta: Get quotes for OM668 Mercedes-Benz A Class engine replacement

entries.title: 2.0L Diesel
entries.history: The OM640 powered the second-generation W169 diesel range, offering improved refinement and lower emissions over the earlier OM668. It was available in multiple power outputs across CDI badged variants.
commonFailures: Timing chain wear and tensioner failure
entries.cta: Get quotes for OM640 Mercedes-Benz A Class engine replacement

entries.title: 1.5L Diesel
entries.history: The OM607 is a Renault-sourced 1.5 dCi engine co-developed with the Renault-Nissan Alliance. It powers entry-level A-Class diesel models and uses a timing belt rather than Mercedes' typical chain .
commonFailures: Timing belt requires replacement every 80,000-100,000 miles
entries.cta: Get quotes for OM607 Mercedes-Benz A Class engine replacement

entries.title: 1.8L / 2.1L Diesel
entries.history: The OM651 is one of Mercedes' most produced diesel engines, known for robust performance and rear-mounted timing chain design. It was fitted to higher-output A-Class diesel variants across multiple generations .
commonFailures: EGR cooler cracking leading to coolant loss
Rear-mounted timing chain tensioner failure (NHTSA recall #14V762)
entries.cta: Get quotes for OM651 Mercedes-Benz A Class engine replacement

entries.title: 2.0L Diesel
entries.history: The OM654 is a clean-sheet design built entirely from aluminium, reducing weight by 17% compared to the OM651. It features steel pistons in an aluminium block for optimised thermal management .
commonFailures: AdBlue system crystallisation and NOx sensor failure
Oil pump seal leaks (early production units)
entries.cta: Get quotes for OM654 Mercedes-Benz A Class engine replacement

entries.title: 1.4L / 1.6L / 2.1L Petrol
entries.history: The M166 family powered the original W168 A-Class, offering compact transverse packaging that enabled the model's revolutionary "sandwich" floorpan design. The A210 Evolution received the largest variant .
entries.cta: Get quotes for M166 Mercedes-Benz A Class engine replacement

entries.title: 1.5L / 1.7L / 2.0L Petrol
entries.history: The M266 improved upon the M166 with better fuel economy and lower emissions, powering the second-generation W169 A-Class across all petrol variants including the A200 Turbo .
entries.cta: Get quotes for M266 Mercedes-Benz A Class engine replacement

entries.title: 1.6L / 2.0L Turbo Petrol
entries.history: The M270 introduced turbocharging across the A-Class petrol range, designed for Mercedes' MFA platform with front-mounted timing chain and direct injection. The A45 AMG received a high-output derivative badged M133 .
commonFailures: Timing chain tensioner failure and chain stretch
entries.cta: Get quotes for M270 Mercedes-Benz A Class engine replacement

entries.title: 2.0L Turbo Petrol
entries.history: The M274 is a longitudinal variant of the M270 family, used in higher-output A-Class models. It shares much of its architecture with the M270 but features revised engine management .
entries.cta: Get quotes for M274 Mercedes-Benz A Class engine replacement

entries.title: 1.3L Turbo Petrol
entries.history: The M282 is a co-developed engine with the Renault-Nissan Alliance (Renault HR13DDT), featuring Mercedes' Nanoslide cylinder coating and cylinder deactivation technology for improved efficiency .
commonFailures: Carbon buildup on intake valves (direct injection)
entries.cta: Get quotes for M282 Mercedes-Benz A Class engine replacement

entries.title: 2.0L Turbo Petrol
entries.history: The M260 replaced the M270 for the W177 generation, producing class-leading specific output. The M139 AMG variant (A45 S) remains one of the most powerful production four-cylinder engines .
entries.cta: Get quotes for M260 Mercedes-Benz A Class engine replacement

entries.title: 1.3L PHEV
entries.history: The A250e combines the M282 petrol engine with an electric motor integrated into the 7-speed DCT gearbox, offering EV-only range and reduced company car tax. The high-voltage battery sits under the rear floor .
entries.cta: Get quotes for M282 PHEV Mercedes-Benz A Class engine replacement

entries.title: 1.5L / 2.0L EQ Boost Mild Hybrid
entries.history: The M264 features Mercedes' EQ Boost mild hybrid system with an integrated starter-generator (RSG) providing 48V assistance for coasting, boost, and energy recovery. It bridged the gap before the M260/M282 introduction .
compatibleVariants: A200 EQ Boost (W177), A220 EQ Boost (W177)
commonFailures: Camshaft lobe wear on early units
entries.cta: Get quotes for M264 Mercedes-Benz A Class engine replacement

commonProblems.h2: Mercedes-Benz A Class Engine Problems - What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense

problems.group: Mercedes-Benz A Class Timing Chain Failure
problems.h4: Mercedes-Benz A Class Timing Chain Failure - Why M270 & OM651 Petrol and Diesel Engines Fail
problems.rootCause: The M270 petrol and OM651 diesel engines use a single timing chain located at the rear of the engine near the gearbox. The plastic chain tensioner guides wear prematurely and the hydraulic tensioner can lose pressure, allowing chain stretch and camshaft timing drift .

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replacement of timing chain, tensioner, guides, sprockets, and camshaft adjuster bolts
repairOptions.longevity: Best done at first rattle (cold start). Prevents catastrophic failure.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete supply & fit of a rebuilt engine with upgraded timing components
repairOptions.longevity: Required if chain has jumped (bent valves). Includes 12-month warranty.

problems.recommendation: If you hear a rattling noise from the rear of the engine on cold starts - especially between 80,000 and 120,000 miles - act immediately. A preventative chain replacement at a specialist saves thousands compared to waiting for failure. Once the chain jumps, piston-to-valve contact is almost certain and engine replacement becomes the only viable option.
problems.cta: Compare rebuilt M270 petrol or OM651 diesel engine prices for your Mercedes-Benz A Class

problems.group: Mercedes-Benz A Class OM651 & OM654 EGR Cooler Failure
problems.h4: Mercedes-Benz A Class OM651 & OM654 EGR Cooler Failure - The #1 Diesel Replacement Driver
problems.rootCause: The exhaust gas recirculation (EGR) cooler develops micro-cracks due to thermal cycling between exhaust heat and coolant flow. Coolant leaks into the exhaust, burns off as white smoke, and can eventually hydrolock the engine if the leak is severe .

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace cracked EGR cooler, flush cooling system, clear fault codes
repairOptions.longevity: Viable if caught early before coolant loss causes engine damage

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete rebuilt engine with upgraded EGR/EGR cooler and new injector seals
repairOptions.longevity: Required if coolant ingestion has bent a rod or washed out a cylinder

problems.recommendation: White smoke on startup, a "sweet" exhaust smell, and frequent low coolant warnings are early warning signs. Replace the EGR cooler at the first symptom - a £900 specialist repair beats a £4,000 replacement engine. On higher-mileage OM651 engines (120k+), consider replacing the timing chain at the same time as the labour overlaps significantly.
problems.cta: Compare quotes for OM651 and OM654 diesel engine replacement for your A-Class

problems.group: Mercedes-Benz A Class M264 & M282 High-Pressure Fuel Pump Failure
problems.h4: Mercedes-Benz A Class M264 & M282 High-Pressure Fuel Pump Failure - Recall 24V-112
problems.rootCause: The high-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) on certain M264 and M282 engines can experience camshaft lobe wear and internal pump failure. Mercedes issued recall 24V-112 for affected vehicles due to potential engine stalling and fuel contamination.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace HPFP, flush fuel system, replace fuel filter, software update
repairOptions.longevity: Must use revised pump with harder camshaft lobe coating

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete engine with revised HPFP, new injectors and fuel rail
repairOptions.longevity: Required if pump failure sent metal fragments through the injection system

problems.recommendation: Check if your A-Class is covered under recall 24V-112 - Mercedes may cover the HPFP replacement even outside warranty. If you're paying out of pocket, ensure any replacement pump has the revised internal coating. Fuel contamination from HPFP failure often kills injectors too; a full fuel system flush is non-negotiable. If the car has driven more than 500 miles with active HPFP failure, engine replacement is often the safer bet.
problems.cta: Compare M264 and M282 rebuilt engine prices for your Mercedes-Benz A-Class

problems.group: Mercedes-Benz A Class AdBlue / SCR System Failure
problems.h4: Mercedes-Benz A Class AdBlue / SCR System Failure - OM651 & OM654 Diesel "No Start" Issues
problems.affectedModels: A180d, A200d, A220d (W176 OM651, W177 OM654); 2014-present with SCR emissions system
problems.rootCause: The AdBlue heater element can fail (P13DF code), the dosing injector can crystallise, or the NOx sensors degrade. The ECU responds with a "No Start in XXX miles" countdown when AdBlue dosing is compromised .

repairOptions.tier: AdBlue Tank Heater or Injector Replacement
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace failed heater element or dosing injector, clear fault codes, perform SCR reset
repairOptions.longevity: Requires specialist diagnostic tools to identify the exact component

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace upstream and/or downstream NOx sensors, ECU software reset
repairOptions.longevity: Common on 2016-2019 models; sensors have a limited service life

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete diesel engine with updated emissions components and injectors
repairOptions.longevity: Only if engine has been run with repeated SCR failures causing EGR clogging

problems.recommendation: When the countdown appears, don't ignore it. A mobile AdBlue specialist (like those in EngineMarket's network) can diagnose and often repair heater or injector faults for half the dealer cost . If you're seeing both P13DF and P20E8 codes simultaneously, start with the heater circuit. For vehicles over 100,000 miles facing repeated SCR issues, compare the cost of an emissions delete (off-road/export only) against replacement.
problems.cta: Compare OM651 and OM654 diesel replacement prices for your A-Class

problems.group: Mercedes-Benz A Class M133 & M139 AMG High-Performance Engine Failure
problems.h4: Mercedes-Benz A Class M133 & M139 AMG High-Performance Engine Failure - A45/A45 S
problems.rootCause: The high-output 2.0-litre turbo engines produce over 200hp per litre. Cylinder 4 runs hottest and can suffer piston ring land failure, cylinder bore scoring, or turbocharger bearing collapse under sustained hard driving, especially without proper warm-up and cool-down.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace turbocharger, oil feed lines, and perform oil flush
repairOptions.longevity: Viable if caught early before debris circulated through engine

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace block, pistons, rings; reuse head ancillaries if undamaged
repairOptions.longevity: For cylinder scoring without head damage

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete rebuilt long engine with upgraded cooling and forged components
repairOptions.longevity: Only option for catastrophic piston failure; requires AMG-specialist builder

problems.recommendation: The M133 is a brilliant but stressed engine. On a W176 A45 worth £12,000-£18,000, a £10,000+ replacement often writes off the car unless you do the work yourself or find a used engine. For M139 A45 S models, the car's value still justifies specialist rebuilding, but always use an AMG-certified specialist - these engines require precision balancing and specific assembly techniques that general rebuilders cannot deliver.
problems.cta: Compare M133 and M139 AMG rebuilt engine prices for Mercedes-Benz A45/A45 S

problems.group: Mercedes-Benz A-Class (W168/W169) M166 Oil Sludge & Overheating
problems.h4: Mercedes-Benz A-Class (W168/W169) M166 Oil Sludge & Overheating - Early Petrol
problems.rootCause: The M166 engine has relatively narrow oil passages that can sludge if oil changes were neglected or extended service intervals were used. Once sludge forms, oil flow to the cylinder head and chain tensioner is restricted, leading to chain noise, head gasket failure, and eventually catastrophic overheating .

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Chemical flush, replace oil pump, cooler, and pickup seal
repairOptions.longevity: Only viable if no knocking and compression is good

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace head gasket, skim head, replace timing chain if accessible
repairOptions.longevity: For coolant loss / overheating without bottom-end damage

repairOptions.tier: Full Replacement - Used or Rebuilt Engine
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete engine replacement with pre-flushed unit
repairOptions.longevity: Most cost-effective option; W168/169 values are low

problems.recommendation: On a W168 or W169 A-Class worth £1,000-£3,000, any major engine repair will exceed the car's value. A used engine from a breaker (£600-£1,200) plus specialist fitting (£800-£1,500) is the most economical path to keeping the car on the road. For owners who love their A-Class, a rebuilt engine with thorough cleaning of oil galleries (£1,500-£2,800) offers a fresh start - but compare quotes carefully before committing.
problems.cta: Compare M166 replacement engine prices for Mercedes-Benz A140/A160/A190

finalCta.h4: Don't Let Engine Failure Write Off Your Mercedes-Benz A Class
finalCta.paragraph: Engine failure doesn't always mean the end of the road. Whether your A-Class needs a preventative timing chain replacement on an OM651 diesel, a recall-compliant HPFP on an M264 petrol, or a full M133 AMG rebuild, EngineMarket makes it simple to compare prices from trusted UK Mercedes-Benz A-Class engine specialists.
finalCta.buttonText: Compare Mercedes-Benz A Class Engine Replacement Prices Now →
finalCta.disclaimer: All prices are indicative UK market ranges based on historical quote data and repair requests from our network of engine specialists. Dealer pricing is estimated based on published main dealer labour rates and genuine OEM parts pricing. Specialist pricing reflects actual quote data from EngineMarket's UK network. Actual costs vary depending on damage severity, vehicle condition, variant, and workshop location within the UK. Use our free comparison tool to get tailored quotes for your specific Mercedes-Benz A Class and engine code. Every engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty - always confirm exact terms with your chosen supplier.

engineSizes.h2: Mercedes-Benz A Class Engines by Size - Find the Right Displacement for Your Variant
engineSizes.intro: Every displacement fitted to the Mercedes-Benz A Class across all generations is listed below, with compatible engine codes, production years and common failure notes to help you identify your exact engine.

groups.title: Mercedes-Benz A Class Diesel Engine Sizes

items.title: Mercedes-Benz A Class 1.5L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.5-litre diesel entered the A-Class range with the W176 generation (2012-2018) as the entry-level A180d, using a Renault-sourced OM607 engine. This unit prioritised fuel economy over performance, returning up to 68.9mpg and CO2 emissions as low as 98g/km, making it popular for company car drivers. It was replaced in the W177 by an updated version of the same displacement.
commonFailurePoints: Timing belt replacement required every 80,000-100,000 miles; injector seal leaks; turbo actuator failure
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes-Benz A Class 1.5L diesel engines

items.title: Mercedes-Benz A Class 1.8L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.8-litre diesel was exclusive to early W176 A-Class models (2012-2015), badged as the A180 CDI and A200 CDI. It utilised the OM651 engine family - Mercedes' own four-cylinder diesel - producing more torque than the 1.5-litre unit while maintaining strong economy figures near 65mpg. This engine was eventually replaced by the 1.5-litre OM607 and the 2.0-litre OM654 in the facelifted and subsequent generations.
commonFailurePoints: EGR cooler cracking leading to coolant loss; rear-mounted timing chain tensioner failure; injector seal leakage
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes-Benz A Class 1.8L diesel engines

items.title: Mercedes-Benz A Class 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.0-litre diesel has been the backbone of the A-Class diesel range across multiple generations. The W169 (2004-2012) used naturally aspirated and turbocharged OM640 units in A160 CDI and A180 CDI models. The OM651 2.1L (often referred to as 2.0L) powered the A200 CDI and A220 CDI in the W176. From 2018, the all-new OM654 2.0-litre launched in the W177 A200d and A220d, offering RDE 2 emissions compliance and up to 65.7mpg.
commonFailurePoints: OM640: timing chain wear; OM651: EGR cooler cracking
timing chain tensioner failure; OM654: AdBlue crystallisation
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes-Benz A Class 2.0L diesel engines

groups.title: Mercedes-Benz A Class Petrol Engine Sizes

items.title: Mercedes-Benz A Class 1.3L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.3-litre turbocharged four-cylinder engine was co-developed with the Renault-Nissan Alliance and debuted in the W177 A-Class (2018-present). Badged as the A180 and A200, this engine replaced the earlier 1.6-litre M270 unit, offering improved efficiency and lower emissions while maintaining competitive power outputs. The M282 engine features cylinder deactivation technology on certain variants to reduce fuel consumption under light loads.
commonFailurePoints: HPFP failure; carbon buildup on intake valves (direct injection); wastegate actuator sticking
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz A Class 1.3L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz A Class 1.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.4-litre petrol engine appeared in the first-generation W168 A-Class (1997-2004), badged as the A140. This M166 unit was naturally aspirated and formed the entry point to the petrol range. It was known for its simplicity and durability when properly maintained, though power was modest even by late-1990s standards.
commonFailurePoints: Head gasket failure; timing chain stretch; camshaft position sensor failure
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz A Class 1.4L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz A Class 1.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.5-litre petrol engine was fitted to the second-generation W169 A-Class (2004-2012) as the A150. This M266 unit offered improved refinement and fuel economy over the earlier M166 family, with a lighter construction and more efficient valvetrain. It was the smallest petrol option in the W169 range, positioned as the entry-level choice.
commonFailurePoints: Variable intake manifold flap issues; oil sludge buildup from extended service intervals
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz A Class 1.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz A Class 1.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.6-litre turbocharged petrol engine debuted with the W176 A-Class (2012-2018) as the A180 and A200, using the M270 engine family. This represented a significant shift to turbocharging across the petrol range, offering diesel-rivalling torque from low RPM. For 2018, the W177 A180 replaced the 1.6-litre unit with the newer 1.3-litre M282, making this displacement exclusive to the previous generation.
commonFailurePoints: chain stretch; high-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) failure; camshaft adjuster solenoid malfunction
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz A Class 1.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz A Class 1.7L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.7-litre petrol engine was available in the W169 A-Class (2004-2012) as the A170. This M266 unit sat between the entry-level 1.5-litre and the larger 2.0-litre petrol options, offering a balanced compromise of performance and efficiency. It was naturally aspirated and proved popular with buyers seeking more power than the A150 without stepping up to the A200.
commonFailurePoints: Variable intake manifold flap issues; oil sludge buildup
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz A Class 1.7L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz A Class 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.0-litre petrol has appeared in multiple A-Class generations across both naturally aspirated and turbocharged forms. The W169 (2004-2012) used a naturally aspirated M266 in the A200. The W176 (2012-2018) introduced turbocharged M270 and M274 units in the A250 and A220, while the W177 (2018-present) continues with the M260 turbo engine in the A250. The AMG variants (A35, A45, A45 S) use high-output derivatives of the same 2.0-litre architecture.
commonFailurePoints: M266: oil sludge; M270/M274: timing chain stretch; M260: turbo wastegate failure; M133/M139: piston ring failure on hard-driven examples
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz A Class 2.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz A Class 2.1L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.1-litre petrol engine was exclusive to the first-generation W168 A-Class (1997-2004) as the range-topping A210 Evolution. This M166 unit featured a larger displacement than the standard 1.6-litre, delivering increased power for the sporty Evolution model. It remains a rare engine in the used market due to the limited production run of the A210 Evolution.
commonFailurePoints: Head gasket failure; timing chain stretch
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz A Class 2.1L petrol engine prices

groups.title: Mercedes-Benz A Class Hybrid / MHEV Engine Sizes

items.title: Mercedes-Benz A Class 1.3L Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV) Engines
items.description: The A250e plug-in hybrid combines the 1.3-litre M282 petrol engine with an electric motor integrated into the 7-speed DCT gearbox. Available exclusively on the W177 generation (2019-present), this powertrain offers an electric-only range suitable for urban commuting while eliminating range anxiety for longer journeys. The high-voltage battery is mounted under the rear floor, preserving boot space.
commonFailurePoints: HV battery degradation over time; electric motor cooling system leaks; high-voltage cabling insulation faults
items.cta: Enquire about Mercedes-Benz A Class 1.3L hybrid engine availability

items.title: Mercedes-Benz A Class 1.5L / 2.0L Mild Hybrid (MHEV) Engines
items.description: The M264 EQ Boost engine combines a 1.5-litre or 2.0-litre petrol unit with a 48V integrated starter-generator (RSG). This mild hybrid system provides coasting, boost assistance, and energy recovery without external charging. It was available on early W177 models (2018-2021) before being phased out in favour of the M260 and M282 families. Not sure which engine size your Mercedes-Benz A Class needs? Enter your registration number above - we'll identify the exact displacement, engine code and compatible replacement options instantly. All engines supplied through EngineMarket include a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty from UK Mercedes-Benz A Class specialists.
commonFailurePoints: 48V battery failure (expensive replacement); HPFP failure (recall 24V-112); camshaft lobe wear on early units
items.cta: Enquire about Mercedes-Benz A Class 1.5L mild hybrid engine availability

engineSizes.closing: Not sure which engine size your Mercedes-Benz A Class needs? Enter your registration number above - we'll identify the exact displacement, engine code and compatible replacement options instantly. All engines supplied through EngineMarket include a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty from UK Mercedes-Benz A Class specialists.

items.title: Mercedes-Benz A Class Diesel Engines
items.description: Diesel power spanned four A-Class generations from 1997 to the present day, with OM600, OM640, OM607, OM651 and OM654 families driving replacement demand - particularly on W176 and W177 models.
items.descriptor: Diesel power spanned four A-Class generations from 1997 to the present day, with OM600, OM640, OM607, OM651 and OM654 families driving replacement demand - particularly on W176 and W177 models.
items.familiesLabel: Common Mercedes-Benz A Class Diesel Engine Families

items.description: Petrol engines have evolved from simple naturally aspirated units (M166, M266) through turbocharged M270/M274 families to the high-output M260 and AMG M133/M139 monsters - spanning W168 to W177 generations.
items.descriptor: Petrol engines have evolved from simple naturally aspirated units (M166, M266) through turbocharged M270/M274 families to the high-output M260 and AMG M133/M139 monsters - spanning W168 to W177 generations.

engineYears.h2: Mercedes-Benz A Class Engines by Year - What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: Every major engine change across the A-Class production timeline from 1997 to 2026, organised so owners can quickly identify their engine and compare rebuilt engine prices from UK specialists.

years.preview: W168 First Generation: M166 Petrol Launch
years.description: The original A-Class introduced Mercedes' first transverse front-wheel drive layout with the revolutionary "sandwich" floorpan - engines slid underneath occupants in a frontal impact .
keyChanges: Launch of the compact MPV-style A-Class (W168)
1998: OM668 common-rail diesel added to range
2001: Facelift with revised suspension and standard ESP (post-moose test)
2002: A210 Evolution flagship with 2.1L M166
mainEngines: OM668 DE17 - 1.7L diesel (A160 CDI / A170 CDI)
knownFor: Revolutionary sandwich floor design - engine goes under occupants in a crash
Failed moose test at launch - ESP became standard on all cars
M166 petrol engines are near-indestructible (300k+ km achievable)
OM668 diesel injector and turbo failures - first-gen common-rail teething issues
years.cta: Get quotes for 1997-2004 Mercedes-Benz A Class replacement engines
years.ticker: Mercedes A140 engine replacement • Mercedes A160 engine reconditioned • Mercedes A170 CDI engine failure • Mercedes A-Class W168 engine price

years.preview: W169 Second Generation: M266 Petrol & OM640 Diesel Evolution
years.description: The second-generation A-Class grew in size and refinement, introducing the A200 Turbo with 193bhp and the reliable OM640 diesel family .
keyChanges: W169 launched with five-door and new three-door body styles
2005: A200 Turbo 2.0L 193bhp petrol added (autumn 2005)
2008: Facelift with stop/start system and Active Park Assist
CVT (AUTOTRONIC) transmission introduced alongside 6-speed manual
BlueEFFICIENCY models with improved aerodynamics
mainEngines: M266 E20 AL - 2.0L turbo petrol (A200 Turbo, 193hp)
OM640 DE20 - 2.0L diesel (A160 CDI / A180 CDI / A200 CDI)
popularModels: Mercedes-Benz A160 CDI / A180 CDI / A200 CDI
knownFor: A200 Turbo delivers 193bhp - the first turbo petrol in A-Class history
OM640 diesel significantly more reliable than OM668 predecessor
CVT transmission (AUTOTRONIC) is the weak point - not the engines
A150 (1.5L) is the most reliable petrol - ignition coil issues after 150k km
years.cta: Compare 2005-2012 Mercedes-Benz A Class engine prices
years.ticker: Mercedes A200 Turbo engine rebuild • Mercedes A180 CDI engine replacement • Mercedes A-Class W169 reconditioned engine • OM640 diesel timing chain wear

years.preview: W176 Third Generation: M270 Turbo Petrol & OM651 Diesel Arrive
years.description: The A-Class transformed from MPV to premium hatchback (W176) - the "sports car" generation with AMG A45 debuting the world's most powerful production four-cylinder engine .
keyChanges: W176 launched - now competes directly with Audi A3 and BMW 1 Series
2013: A45 AMG launched with M133 - 360hp (later 381hp)
2015: Facelift with revised styling and updated engines
4MATIC all-wheel drive available on A250 and A220d
1.5L OM607 diesel (Renault-sourced) added for A180d
mainEngines: M133 DE20 - 2.0L AMG turbo petrol (A45 AMG, 360-381hp)
OM651 - 1.8L/2.1L diesel (A180 CDI / A200 CDI / A220 CDI, 109-177hp)
knownFor: A45 AMG M133 - most powerful 2.0L production engine (380hp)
M270 timing chain tensioner failure - cold start rattle from rear of engine
OM651 EGR cooler cracking - coolant loss between 60k-100k miles
W176 marked the A-Class coming of age as a true premium hatchback
years.cta: Get quotes for 2013-2018 Mercedes-Benz A Class replacement engines
years.ticker: Mercedes A180 engine replacement • Mercedes A45 AMG engine rebuild • Mercedes A200d engine failure • M270 timing chain replacement cost • OM651 EGR cooler repair

years.preview: W177 Fourth Generation: M282 Petrol, OM654 Diesel & Electrification
years.description: The W177 introduced the M282 1.3L petrol (co-developed with Renault), the refined OM654 2.0L diesel, and electrified powertrains including the A250e plug-in hybrid .
keyChanges: All-new MFA2 platform with larger dimensions and rear legroom
2018: A250e plug-in hybrid launched (M282 + electric motor)
2019: A35 AMG with M260 2.0L turbo (306hp)
2019: A45 S AMG with M139 - 416hp (later 421hp), world's most powerful 2.0L
2023: Final facelift before 2026 discontinuation
Production ends 2026 - A-Class to be discontinued globally

M139 DE20 - 2.0L AMG turbo petrol (A45 387hp / A45 S 416-421hp)
popularModels: Mercedes-Benz A35 AMG / A45 AMG / A45 S AMG
knownFor: M282 co-developed with Renault - reliable but HPFP failures reported
OM654 diesel - refined and efficient but AdBlue system issues common
A45 S M139 - 421hp from 2.0L - F1-inspired technology
M260 turbo wastegate rattle - known weak point on early cars
M264 48V mild hybrid battery failure - expensive repair (£1.5k-£2.5k)
The final A-Class generation before discontinuation (2026)

faq.h2: Mercedes-Benz A Class Engine Replacement - Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: How much does a Mercedes-Benz A Class engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A replacement engine for a Mercedes-Benz A Class typically costs between £1,200 and £15,000 depending on the engine code, generation and condition. Used units start from £800-£2,500, rebuilt from £1,800-£5,500, and AMG engines from £8,000-£15,000.
keyPoints: Used A180d (OM607) engines: £800-£1,500 depending on mileage
Rebuilt M270 petrol units (A180/A200): £1,600-£3,200 supply only
Rebuilt OM651 diesel units (A200 CDI/A220 CDI): £2,500-£4,800
A45 AMG M133 rebuilt engines: £8,000-£12,000 from specialists
Labour for supply & fit adds £400-£800 to any engine price
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz A Class engine replacement prices from UK specialists

items.question: Which Mercedes-Benz A Class engines have timing chain problems?
items.answer: The M270 petrol (2012-2018 A180/A200/A250) and OM651 diesel (2012-2018 A180 CDI/A200 CDI/A220 CDI) engines are most known for timing chain tensioner failure. The chain sits at the rear of the engine, making replacement labour-intensive.
keyPoints: M270 timing chain rattle typically appears between 80,000-150,000 miles
OM651 rear-mounted chain requires gearbox removal for replacement
Preventative chain replacement costs £1,400-£2,200 at a specialist
Catastrophic chain failure causes bent valves - replacement engine needed
Post-2018 M260/M282 and OM654 engines have improved chain designs
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes-Benz A Class timing chain replacement

items.question: Is the Mercedes-Benz A Class OM651 diesel engine reliable?
items.answer: The OM651 is generally robust but has two well-documented weak points: EGR cooler cracking and timing chain tensioner failure. Post-2014 models received updated components that improved reliability, but preventative maintenance remains essential.
keyPoints: EGR cooler cracks cause coolant loss and white smoke from exhaust
HPFP failure on early OM651 units documented in TSB 07.01.15
Post-2014 models include fixes for pre-2014 common flaws
Regular oil changes every 10,000 miles minimise chain wear
Many specialists offer upgraded EGR coolers with reinforced brazing
items.cta: Compare OM651 diesel replacement engines for your A-Class

items.question: What is the most reliable Mercedes-Benz A Class engine to buy used?
items.answer: The M270 1.6L petrol (2012-2018 A180/A200) is widely considered the safest A-Class engine for used buyers. It has no AdBlue system, fewer emissions components than diesels, and timing chain issues are predictable and repairable before failure.
keyPoints: M270 petrol avoids DPF, AdBlue and EGR cooler failure risks of diesels
A200 petrol returns 48.7mpg - competitive with diesel for mixed driving
OM607 diesel (A180d) is Renault-sourced and timing belt needs 80k-100k changes
Post-2018 M282 petrol co-developed with Renault - generally reliable
Avoid early OM651 diesels (2012-2014) with unresolved HPFP issues
items.cta: Find a reconditioned Mercedes-Benz A Class petrol engine with warranty

items.question: How long does a Mercedes-Benz A Class engine last?
items.answer: A well-maintained A-Class petrol engine typically lasts 150,000-200,000 miles, while diesels regularly exceed 200,000-250,000 miles. The OM651 diesel in particular is capable of 300,000+ miles when serviced properly and timing chain issues are addressed proactively.
keyPoints: M270 petrol: 150k-200k miles with regular oil changes
OM651 diesel: 200k-300k miles - timing chain is the limiting factor
OM654 diesel (2018+): Too new for full data, but build quality is excellent
M282 petrol (2018+): 150k+ miles expected, HPFP is weak point
Critical factors: Oil changes every 10k miles, not ignoring warning signs
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes-Benz A Class replacement engines at any mileage

items.question: What causes the Mercedes-Benz A Class M270 engine to lose power?
items.answer: Power loss on M270 engines is most commonly caused by timing chain stretch (causing cam timing drift), HPFP failure, or boost pressure sensor issues. A 2014 A200 owner on MBClub reported struggling past 70mph with no fault codes - intermittent boost sensor faults are notoriously hard to diagnose.
keyPoints: Stretched timing chain allows camshaft timing to drift - reduces power
HPFP failure causes misfires and limp mode without warning codes
Boost pressure sensor faults can be intermittent and code-free
Camshaft adjuster solenoid failure also causes power loss
Diagnostic with Mercedes STAR system is essential for accurate fault finding
items.cta: Compare M270 engine prices for your A180, A200 or A250

items.question: Is the Mercedes-Benz A Class A250e plug-in hybrid engine reliable?
items.answer: The A250e PHEV (M282 petrol + electric motor) has no recurrent reported failures according to European reliability studies. The 1.3L petrol unit is proven, and the electric motor integrated into the gearbox has been reliable. Battery degradation over time is the main long-term consideration.
keyPoints: No chronic failures reported on the hybrid system as of 2024
Electric-only range of 44 miles (WLTP) - battery health check recommended
HV battery replacement is expensive (£3,000-£5,000) if out of warranty
High-voltage system requires EV-trained specialist for engine work
Cooling system leaks reported on some early electric motor units
items.cta: Enquire about A250e plug-in hybrid engine availability

items.question: Should I replace or repair my Mercedes-Benz A Class engine?
items.answer: Repair if the issue is timing chain or EGR cooler caught early. Replace if the engine has suffered timing chain snap (bent valves), HPFP failure that sent metal through the fuel system, or piston ring failure on AMG models. For A-Class cars worth under £3,000, a used engine swap often beats major repairs.
keyPoints: Preventative timing chain replacement (£1,400-£2,200) beats engine replacement (£2,500-£5,500)
EGR cooler replacement (£500-£900) prevents hydrolock damage
HPFP metal contamination requires full engine replacement - flushing won't guarantee safety
On W168/W169 worth £1k-£3k, used engine + fitting is most economical
A45 AMG M133 rebuild (£8k-£12k) - compare against vehicle value
items.cta: Get expert advice on Mercedes-Benz A Class repair vs replacement

items.question: What are the common problems with Mercedes-Benz A Class diesel engines?
items.answer: The OM651 (2012-2018) suffers EGR cooler cracking, timing chain tensioner wear, and HPFP failure. The OM654 (2018+) has AdBlue crystallisation and NOx sensor failures. The OM607 (A180d) has a timing belt needing replacement every 80,000-100,000 miles and injector seal leaks.
keyPoints: OM651 EGR cooler cracks between 60k-100k miles - coolant loss, white smoke
OM654 AdBlue system faults trigger countdown-to-no-start messages
OM607 timing belt is an interference engine - failure means catastrophic damage
DPF blockage common on all diesels used for short journeys only
Post-2014 OM651 units have improved HPFP and chain tensioner designs
items.cta: Compare diesel engine prices for your Mercedes-Benz A Class

items.question: How do I find the correct engine code for my Mercedes-Benz A Class?
items.answer: Your A-Class engine code is in the vehicle service booklet, on a sticker in the driver's door shut, or stamped on the engine block. You can also enter your registration number into our tool above for instant identification. Common A-Class codes include M270, M282, OM651, OM654 and OM607.
keyPoints: Check service booklet or driver's door sill sticker for "M" or "OM" code
Engine block stamp visible near cylinder head or oil filter housing
VIN decoder tools can identify original factory engine code
items.cta: Enter your reg above to instantly identify your Mercedes-Benz A Class engine code
